eli3: Partial derivatives measure how a function changes when you vary one variable while holding others constant. For f(x,y), ∂f/∂x tells you the rate of change moving in the x-direction, keeping y fixed.

Think of a hiking trail on a mountain. The partial derivative with respect to x tells you the slope if you walk due east. With respect to y, it's the slope walking due north. Together they describe the local terrain.

The gradient collects all partial derivatives into a vector. It points in the direction of steepest ascent. Mixed partials (∂²f/∂x∂y) tell you how the slope in one direction changes as you move in another.

whenWhy.title: Computing gradients for optimization
whenWhy.body: Each partial ∂f/∂xᵢ is a component of the gradient ∇f. Optimization algorithms need all partials to know which direction to step. Automatic differentiation computes these efficiently via backpropagation.

whenWhy.title: Sensitivity analysis and feature importance
whenWhy.body: ∂(prediction)/∂(feature) tells you how sensitive the model output is to each input feature. Large partial derivative = important feature. Used in explainable AI (saliency maps, integrated gradients).

whenWhy.title: Hessian and curvature analysis
whenWhy.body: Second partials form the Hessian matrix Hᵢⱼ = ∂²f/∂xᵢ∂xⱼ. Determines convexity (H positive semi-definite), critical point type (min, max, saddle), and convergence rate of optimization.

whenWhy.title: PDEs and physics
whenWhy.body: Partial differential equations (heat equation, wave equation, Navier-Stokes) model how quantities vary in space and time. Each term involves partial derivatives with respect to different variables.

deepDiveIntro: For f: ℝⁿ → ℝ, the partial derivative with respect to xᵢ is ∂f/∂xᵢ = lim_{h→0} [f(x₁,…,xᵢ+h,…,xₙ) - f(x)]/h. It's computed by treating all variables except xᵢ as constants and differentiating with respect to xᵢ.

Clairaut's theorem (Schwarz's theorem): if second partials are continuous, then ∂²f/∂xᵢ∂xⱼ = ∂²f/∂xⱼ∂xᵢ. The Hessian matrix is symmetric. Total derivative combines partials with differentials: df = Σᵢ (∂f/∂xᵢ)dxᵢ = ∇fᵀdx.

deepDive.heading: Definition and Computation
deepDive.body: Partial derivative ∂f/∂xᵢ computed by holding other variables constant. Notation: f_{xᵢ}, ∂_{xᵢ}f, fᵢ. Geometrically, it's the slope of the tangent line in the xᵢ-direction (intersecting the surface with a plane holding other variables fixed).

deepDive.heading: Gradient as Vector of Partials
deepDive.body: ∇f = [∂f/∂x₁, …, ∂f/∂xₙ]ᵀ collects all first partials. Directional derivative D_vf = ∇fᵀv = Σ vᵢ(∂f/∂xᵢ). The gradient is orthogonal to level sets and points in direction of maximum increase.

deepDive.heading: Higher-Order Partials
deepDive.body: Second partials: ∂²f/∂xᵢ² (pure) and ∂²f/∂xᵢ∂xⱼ (mixed). Hessian Hᵢⱼ = ∂²f/∂xᵢ∂xⱼ. When continuous, mixed partials commute (Clairaut's theorem). Higher-order partials used in Taylor expansions and optimization analysis.

deepDive.heading: Total Derivative
deepDive.body: If x depends on t, total derivative df/dt accounts for all paths: df/dt = Σᵢ (∂f/∂xᵢ)(dxᵢ/dt). This is the chain rule. Differential form: df = Σᵢ (∂f/∂xᵢ)dxᵢ = ∇fᵀdx. Exact differential if f is continuously differentiable.

deepDive.heading: Implicit Differentiation
deepDive.body: For F(x,y) = 0 defining y implicitly as function of x, differentiate both sides: ∂F/∂x + (∂F/∂y)(dy/dx) = 0, so dy/dx = -(∂F/∂x)/(∂F/∂y). Extends to multiple variables via implicit function theorem.

takeaways: Partial derivative varies one variable, holds others constant.
Gradient ∇f is vector of all first partials; points in direction of steepest ascent.
Total derivative df/dt = Σ (∂f/∂xᵢ)(dxᵢ/dt) via chain rule.
Implicit differentiation uses partials to find derivatives of implicitly defined functions.

edgeCases.q: When do mixed partials not commute?
edgeCases.a: If second partials are not continuous, mixed partials may differ. Classic example: f(x,y) = xy(x²-y²)/(x²+y²) for (x,y)≠(0,0), f(0,0)=0. Then ∂²f/∂x∂y(0,0) = -1 but ∂²f/∂y∂x(0,0) = 1. Continuity of second partials guarantees equality (Clairaut's theorem).

edgeCases.q: Can partial derivatives exist when the function isn't differentiable?
edgeCases.a: Yes. Example: f(x,y) = |x| has partial ∂f/∂x at (0,y) undefined, but f(x,y) = |x| + |y| has both partials equal to 0 at (0,0) yet isn't differentiable there (corners in both directions). Existence of all partials ≠ differentiability.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between ∂ and d?
edgeCases.a: ∂ indicates partial derivative (holding other variables constant). d indicates total derivative (accounting for all dependencies). For f(x(t),y(t)), ∂f/∂t doesn't make sense (f doesn't directly depend on t), but df/dt = ∂f/∂x · dx/dt + ∂f/∂y · dy/dt.
